description:
You are working as a database-reliability engineer.  
    A directory already exists at  

        /home/user/db_backups  

    containing several plain-text PostgreSQL dump files produced with `pg_dump` and named in the format  

        backup_YYYYMMDD.sql  

    1. Your goal is to archive every SQL dump that was created in **January 2022** (i.e. files whose name matches the shell-glob `*_202201*.sql`).  
       • Place the resulting compressed archive in  

            /home/user/backup_archives  

         and name the archive file exactly  

            202201_backups.tgz  

    2. At the same time, create a plain-text **log file** alongside the archive, named  

            202201_backups.log  

       The log file must list the full, absolute path of each file that you added to the archive, **one file per line, no extra spaces or blank lines**.  
       Example of the required format (note the trailing new-line after the last entry as well):  
            /home/user/db_backups/backup_20220115.sql  
            /home/user/db_backups/backup_20220120.sql⏎  

       The automated test will compare the log file byte-for-byte with this expected layout, so make sure the paths are correct and that there is a single “\n” (U+000A) after each line, including the final one.

    3. Use GNU `find` piped to `xargs` (or `-exec`) so that all work is done in **one short command pipeline of at most three simple commands** (for example, `find … | xargs tar …`).  
       You may create `/home/user/backup_archives` if it does not already exist, but do not delete or rename any of the original dump files.

    SUCCESS CRITERIA (checked automatically):  
       • File “/home/user/backup_archives/202201_backups.tgz” exists.  
       • The archive contains exactly two members:  
            backup_20220115.sql  
            backup_20220120.sql  
         (The order inside the archive is not important.)  
       • File “/home/user/backup_archives/202201_backups.log” exists and matches the precise format described above.

    Remember: the grading script only checks the final filesystem state; it does **not** look at your terminal output.
truth:
<!-- Pre-existing data placed in the image before the agent starts -->
    <preexisting>
        <directory path="/home/user/db_backups"/>
        <file path="/home/user/db_backups/backup_20220115.sql">-- dummy SQL dump for 15 Jan 2022</file>
        <file path="/home/user/db_backups/backup_20220120.sql">-- dummy SQL dump for 20 Jan 2022</file>
        <file path="/home/user/db_backups/backup_20220201.sql">-- dummy SQL dump for 01 Feb 2022</file>
        <file path="/home/user/db_backups/backup_20220210.sql">-- dummy SQL dump for 10 Feb 2022</file>
    </preexisting>

    <!-- Expected artefacts the agent must create -->
    <expected>
        <!-- Archive file -->
        <file path="/home/user/backup_archives/202201_backups.tgz">
            TAR-GZ archive, gzip-compressed, containing exactly:
                backup_20220115.sql
                backup_20220120.sql
        </file>

        <!-- Log file -->
        <file path="/home/user/backup_archives/202201_backups.log">
/home/user/db_backups/backup_20220115.sql
/home/user/db_backups/backup_20220120.sql
        </file>
    </expected>
name: task_000000_fa332d03
